picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA posted Q1 FY2027 record revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY) with $49B FCF and guided Q2 at $91B ±2%. The stock now trades at ~19.3x forward P/E — below the S&P 500 average — after digesting recent semiconductor sector stress, while the Vera Rubin architecture ramp accelerates AI infrastructure demand. Today's Micron blowout (+15.6%) directly validates the AI hardware spend cycle that underpins NVDA's demand pipeline, and 62 analysts maintain a Strong Buy with a $298.93 target implying ~50% upside from ~$200.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings on August 26, 2026 — Vera Rubin ramp and $91B revenue guidance confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$200, off the $236.54 ATH; forward P/E ~19.3x is below the S&P 500 average and well below the 5Y semi average — one of the rare moments NVDA screens as reasonably valued on forward numbers; stabilizing above $196 support post-sector selloff
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 72% or a hyperscaler announcing meaningful capex reduction — specifically Blackwell design win loss at a top-4 cloud provider
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export control expansion to cover Blackwell-class chips; China revenue permanently impaired following H20 blacklisting

picks.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron just delivered a historic fiscal Q3 blowout — revenue ~$41.5B vs. mid-$35B consensus and EPS ~$25 vs. ~$20 expected — with Q4 guidance of $30–$32 EPS, triggering a wave of price target raises to $1,400–$2,000 from TD Cowen, BofA, Wedbush, Barclays, and DA Davidson. HBM4 supply is fully booked for 2026 and shipping to NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform, while HBM capacity constraints are lifting conventional DRAM prices across the stack. As the only U.S. company manufacturing HBM at scale, MU carries a geopolitical moat reinforced by CHIPS Act subsidies and domestic manufacturing mandates — thesis intact and materially strengthened today.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings guidance print + continued HBM4 ramp confirmation into Vera Rubin cycle
picks.catalystWindow: Next 1–2 weeks (guidance digestion and analyst revision wave); Q4 report in ~90 days
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$1,213 post-earnings surge, well above prior resistance near $1,000; forward P/E ~18.4x despite 665% YoY EPS growth — one of the few mega-cap AI names that screens cheap on forward earnings relative to sector median
picks.exitTrigger: HBM pricing softening due to SK Hynix or Samsung supply surge; revenue growth decelerating below 30% YoY; Goldman Sachs' cyclical mean-reversion thesis materializing with DRAM oversupply signal
picks.keyRisk: Memory is cyclical — coordinated capacity expansion by Samsung and SK Hynix could compress margins sharply within 12–18 months

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's Q2 FY2026 delivered $22.19B revenue (+48% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue surging 143% YoY, representing 68% of total revenue, and record FCF of $10.3B. Morningstar rates AVGO as moderately undervalued — a rare analyst read given its scale — and the stock has outperformed NVDA on a 12-month basis (+82% vs +69%). The VMware integration provides recurring software revenue that diversifies away from pure semiconductor cyclicality, making AVGO the highest-quality AI infrastructure compounder in the universe with 67.3% operating margins.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (expected ~September 2026) — consensus expects continued AI segment >140% growth and updated XPU custom chip guidance
picks.entryNote: Trading at a modest premium to fair value post Q2 dip; the -13% earnings-day reaction despite a clean beat created a better entry; Morningstar 'moderately undervalued' read at current levels is the key valuation anchor
picks.exitTrigger: AI custom chip (XPU) revenue growth decelerating below 80% YoY, or hyperscaler capex freeze reducing ASIC design win pipeline materially
picks.keyRisk: Market continues to demand perfect guidance execution; any capex softening from top hyperscaler customers triggers multiple compression on elevated expectations

picks.name: Meta Platforms, Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta is the strongest risk-adjusted hyperscaler in the book — its Advantage+ AI ad platform generates prolific FCF even while investing $60–70B in annual AI capex, a self-funding quality profile no other hyperscaler matches. FY estimate revisions have been sharply upward following Q1 AI ad monetization results, and the stock screens well on quality + earnings revision momentum. The macro backdrop (sticky inflation, resilient consumer services spend) supports digital ad pricing, while AI-driven targeting efficiency widens Meta's moat against linear TV and programmatic competitors.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Advantage+ AI advertising revenue metrics and Reality Labs hardware update
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$578; the sector rotation from hyperscaler capex spenders to monetizers favors META over MSFT or AMZN in the near-term; advertising is earlier in the AI monetization cycle than cloud infrastructure
picks.exitTrigger: Digital advertising revenue growth decelerating below 15% YoY; FTC antitrust action forcing Instagram/WhatsApp divestiture; Reality Labs losses accelerating above $20B annual run rate
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory overhang — EU/FTC actions remain live; any ad revenue miss in a slowing consumer environment would compress the multiple quickly

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet is 'literally firing on all cylinders' — Google Cloud grew +63% in Q1 2026 (accelerating from +48% in Q4 2025), Search monetization remains dominant, and Berkshire under Greg Abel nearly tripled its stake (+225% increase to ~57.8M shares / ~$16.6B) in Q1 2026, the clearest smart money validation in the universe. The newly confirmed DJIA addition (replacing Verizon before June 29) creates mechanical index-buying demand. Analyst upgrades tied to agentic AI capabilities reinforce the structural monetization story.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Google Cloud revenue growth rate vs. 63% Q1 benchmark; agentic AI monetization metrics
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$367; Berkshire's $16.6B position at roughly this level signals a value floor from the world's most respected long-term capital allocator; DJIA inclusion triggers passive accumulation before June 29
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerating below 40% YoY or DOJ antitrust remedy forcing Search business separation
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedies — a structural breakup of Search or Chrome would permanently impair the advertising moat and ad/cloud cross-sell engine

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman is the strongest non-tech multi-factor story in the book. Q1 2026 EPS materially beat estimates on capital markets revival (M&A, IPOs reopening), asset management fee growth, and trading revenue. The steep yield curve (10Y-2Y at +97 bps) is a structural NIM and trading revenue tailwind, and the Marcus consumer banking drag is fully absorbed. FY2026 estimates have been revised sharply upward as deal flow accelerates — the IPO/M&A pipeline is the widest it has been since 2021. Tepper (Appaloosa), Druckenmiller (Duquesne's XLF exit was tactical, not sector-bearish), and institutions broadly are rotating into financials as the AI-capex GDP impact fuels advisory revenues.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — investment banking revenue and M&A/IPO league table confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~14x forward P/E vs. 13x median — a modest premium justified by ROIC inflection; bear-steepening yield curve is an ongoing tailwind; technically consolidating near YTD highs
picks.exitTrigger: 10Y–2Y spread compressing below +50 bps (curve flattening); credit quality deterioration in leveraged loan book; regulatory capital requirement increase above Basel III endgame proposals
picks.keyRisk: A hard landing scenario collapses M&A/IPO volumes — Goldman's capital markets revenues are highly volatile and leveraged to deal activity

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality compounder in the financial sector — a toll-road on global capital markets with 70%+ EBITDA margins, pricing power reinforced by regulatory moats, and recurring revenue from ratings, data analytics, and index licensing. In a late-cycle environment with bear-steepening (debt issuance picking up, corporate refinancing cycles active), Ratings revenue accelerates. The AI era adds a secular tailwind through data/analytics demand. SPGI has beaten estimates in 4 of 4 recent quarters with upward FY revisions, making it the most durable compounder outside tech in the book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — ratings revenue growth driven by investment-grade debt issuance volumes in the bear-steepening environment
picks.entryNote: Trading at a modest premium to its 5Y P/E average but justified by margin expansion and secular data/analytics demand; has held up better than the broader market in recent semi-sector volatility
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapsing on a hard landing; antitrust action forcing structural separation of ratings and data businesses; revenue growth decelerating below 8% YoY
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory overhang — DOJ review of financial data duopolies (SPGI/MSCI) remains a latent risk that could force business segment restructuring

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier aerospace & defense compounder in the portfolio — it spans both commercial aerospace (Pratt & Whitney engine MRO cycle) and defense (Raytheon missiles, radar systems) in one balance sheet. Defense budgets are structurally rising globally as NATO allies hit 2%+ GDP targets post-Russia-Ukraine, and commercial aerospace aftermarket revenue is a compounding toll-road on the installed engine base. Industrial stocks like RTX are benefiting from increased capital spending on defense and AI infrastructure buildout adjacent supply chains. The sector rotation data confirms Industrials remain Tier 1 despite RS flattening at the index level — single-name quality picks like RTX remain in clear uptrends.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Pratt & Whitney GTF engine production ramp and defense backlog update; any NATO capex announcement
picks.entryNote: Trading at a modest forward P/E premium vs. historical average, justified by defense super-cycle; held in the portfolio every day of the last 7 sessions — thesis fully intact
picks.exitTrigger: Iran nuclear deal fully resolving and collapsing the geopolitical risk premium embedded in defense spending; Pratt & Whitney GTF engine recall costs exceeding $10B cumulative
picks.keyRisk: A comprehensive Iran peace deal (within the 2-month window disclosed) could reduce the geopolitical risk premium driving defense procurement urgency

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Energy has quietly taken the #1 relative strength slot across all timeframes, with XOM as the anchor — up 22%+ YTD with the strongest institutional inflows of any old-economy sector. PCE coming in at 4.1% (highest since April 2023) directly reinforces the inflation/real asset hedge thesis. XOM's Permian Basin scale, Pioneer integration synergies, and LNG export position give it the most durable FCF profile in upstream energy. Oil falling to pre-war levels on Iran deal hopes is a near-term headwind, but the structural case — PPI at 6%, real rates barely positive, commodity equities as inflation hedge — remains intact for the 1–5Y horizon.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Pioneer synergy realization, Permian production volumes, and updated oil price deck sensitivity
picks.catalystWindow: Late July 2026 (4–5 weeks); also watch Iran deal timeline (2-month window from June)
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~12x forward P/E vs. 13x historical median — fair-to-cheap; oil price decline from Iran deal speculation creates a better entry than 2 weeks ago; medium conviction given Iran tail risk on near-term oil prices
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained Brent crude below $70/barrel on Iran deal completion + OPEC+ supply restoration; XOM Permian production growth missing guidance by >10%
picks.keyRisk: A fully resolved Iran nuclear deal delivering a deflationary oil shock — the single biggest near-term macro tailwind for equities broadly but a direct headwind for energy sector earnings

picks.name: Quanta Services, Inc.
picks.rationale: Quanta is the premier electrical infrastructure contractor in the U.S. — a direct beneficiary of the AI data center buildout, grid modernization, and renewable energy transmission. The $754B AI capex consensus for 2026 must physically arrive at data centers via electrical transmission infrastructure, and PWR holds the most critical skilled-labor and permitting moats in that supply chain. Industrial stocks are being supported by capital spending on electricity capacity and construction around the AI infrastructure buildout. Held in the portfolio every day of the last 7 sessions — thesis intact and strengthening as PCE at 4.1% keeps the 'real asset/infrastructure' rotation alive.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — data center electrification project backlog and margin expansion confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at a modest premium to its historical P/E given the secular infrastructure capex cycle; technically strong — in a sustained uptrend with institutional accumulation from the AI-infrastructure rotation; held all 7 prior sessions with no thesis degradation
picks.exitTrigger: AI hyperscaler capex freeze reducing data center construction pipeline >25%; labor cost inflation compressing EBITDA margins below 10%
picks.keyRisk: AI capex ROI crisis causing hyperscalers to pause or cut data center construction — PWR's backlog is concentrated in a handful of mega-projects that could be deferred

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the most direct hedge against today's specific combination of risks: PCE at 4.1% (highest since April 2023), real policy rates barely positive (~+0.3% nominal, now negative on PCE basis), a fiscal deficit running 6–7% of GDP, and a geopolitical backdrop with Iran conflict risk premium still embedded. Today's hotter-than-expected PCE print directly validates the 'sticky inflation / negative real rate' thesis for gold. Held as the #1 defensive anchor every session this week.
picks.catalyst: Monthly PCE releases and any Fed communication shift — a PCE print above 4.5% or a July FOMC hike surprise would be immediate gold catalysts
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — next major catalyst is July FOMC pricing shift in next 2–4 weeks
picks.entryNote: GLD ~$428; technically in a sustained uptrend supported by the inflation regime; real rates on a PCE basis are now slightly negative — the historical sweet spot for gold outperformance
picks.exitTrigger: Real policy rates turning definitively positive (Fed funds >5.0% with core PCE sub-3.0%) or a comprehensive Iran deal collapsing the geopolitical risk premium
picks.keyRisk: A sudden disinflation shock (e.g., oil collapse on Iran deal + demand destruction) could temporarily compress gold's inflation premium

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD screens for dividend growth, FCF coverage, and ROE — delivering a low-beta portfolio of compounders across energy, financials, industrials, and consumer staples that directly mirrors the sector rotation thesis. In a late-cycle stagflationary environment where stretched AI multiples face compression, SCHD's ~3.5–4% yield, sub-1.0 beta, and value-tilted composition provide genuine ballast. The 'old economy taking the baton' rotation thesis makes SCHD an active expression of the macro regime, not just a defensive hedge.
picks.catalyst: Ongoing dividend reinvestment + potential market rotation into income/value names if AI capex ROI concerns accelerate
picks.catalystWindow: Evergreen; most relevant in next 6–12 months if tech multiple compression deepens
picks.entryNote: Current yield ~3.5–4%; trading at a P/E discount to SPY; provides direct exposure to energy, financials, and industrials — the top-ranked sectors — via a diversified, low-cost vehicle
picks.exitTrigger: A decisive Fed pivot to rate cuts compressing the income premium; SPY forward P/E compressing to 15–16x making broad equity more attractive than dividend-screened subsets
picks.keyRisk: Dividend coverage deterioration in a hard-landing scenario where FCF at constituent companies compresses across the board

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B holds ~$397B in cash/T-bills (earning ~$18B/year risk-free), owns BNSF, GEICO, Berkshire Energy, and a $263B equity portfolio anchored in AAPL, BAC, and now GOOGL (newly tripled to $16.6B). Under CEO Greg Abel, Q1 2026 showed decisive active management — the GOOGL tripling, new DAL position, and Visa/Mastercard exits signal a more active capital deployment posture. The $397B cash hoard is an automatic stabilizer: when markets fall, Berkshire deploys, creating an asymmetric payoff profile in a risk-off scenario. Held every session this week as the core defensive compounder.
picks.catalyst: Any major market dislocation enabling Berkshire to deploy its $397B cash hoard at depressed valuations; Q2 2026 13F filing (August 14) may reveal additional Greg Abel-era strategic moves
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.5x book value — reasonable vs. history; cash pile alone (~59% of investable portfolio) provides a margin of safety; the free ~$18B/year T-bill income stream underpins EPS even in a downturn
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel making a major acquisition at a dilutive price (>2x book of target) or catastrophic insurance event depleting the capital buffer below $200B
picks.keyRisk: Opportunity cost risk — if the AI bull market continues another 2–3 years, the $397B cash pile earns 4–5% while equities compound at 15%+, creating a relative underperformance narrative

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF (7–10 year duration) offers the optimal Treasury hedge: enough duration to benefit materially from a Fed pivot or growth scare, without TLT's extreme sensitivity to the fiscal/bond-vigilante scenario where long-end yields spike on deficit concerns. Current yield ~4.3–4.4% provides genuine carry while functioning as portfolio insurance against a macro slowdown. Today's hot PCE print (4.1%) argues for smaller sizing vs. GLD, but the growth deceleration signal (Q1 GDP revised to 1.6% from 2.0%) maintains the rationale for some duration exposure.
picks.catalyst: Any labor market softening signal (unemployment >4.5%) or Fed pivot communication that reprices the front end lower
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3–6 months — July FOMC and Q3 labor data are the key catalysts
picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at ~4.49% is near the upper end of the 2026 range; entering IEF near these yields captures reasonable carry with asymmetric upside if growth disappoints; avoid TLT given fiscal tail risk on the long end
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaking above 5.25% on a sustained basis (bond vigilante scenario) or core PCE accelerating above 4.5% cementing a hike cycle
picks.keyRisk: A sustained bond vigilante episode driven by fiscal concerns could push the 10Y above 5.25%, delivering a -5 to -8% drawdown on IEF before the growth hedge pays off

picks.name: Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: Coca-Cola is the canonical consumer staples anchor — a global pricing-power compounder with 60%+ gross margins, consistent dividend growth (60+ year Dividend King), and near-zero correlation to AI capex narratives. In a late-cycle stagflationary environment, KO's pricing power means revenue holds even as volumes soften — it passes inflation through to consumers better than most industrials or tech names. As the defensive sleeve needs a consumer staples exposure per the diversification mandate, KO at a reasonable forward multiple (~23x) is preferable to overvalued WMT/COST (Morningstar 1-star) and provides dividend income that partially offsets IEF duration risk.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — pricing realization vs. volume elasticity metrics; any dividend increase announcement
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~23x forward P/E — modest premium to its 5Y average of 21–22x, but justified in a high-inflation regime where pricing power is the scarcest commodity; technically stable, held on June 22 and June 24 in prior book
picks.exitTrigger: Volume declines accelerating beyond -5% YoY (pricing power exhausted by consumer pushback); free cash flow coverage of dividend falling below 1.2x
picks.keyRisk: Consumer trade-down to private label accelerating beyond projections; GLP-1 drug adoption reducing sugary beverage consumption at a faster rate than priced into consensus

summary: The regime call remains Cautiously Bullish / Late-Cycle Stagflationary: PCE printing at 4.1% today — the hottest since April 2023 — reinforces the hawkish Fed hold, negative real rates on a PCE basis, and the 'old economy + AI infrastructure' rotation thesis that has anchored this book all week. The 10 growth picks are structured around two core themes: (1) AI infrastructure compounders with durable earnings (NVDA, MU — powerfully validated by today's blowout Q3, AVGO, GOOGL, META) where forward P/Es have normalized to reasonable levels despite explosive growth, and (2) quality non-tech compounders exposed to the bear-steepening and capex supercycle (GS, SPGI, RTX, XOM, PWR) that hedge AI concentration risk. Carry-overs are strong: NVDA, META, GOOGL, AVGO, GS, SPGI, RTX, XOM, and PWR all held from prior sessions with theses intact; MU is elevated from defensive/secondary to a top-2 conviction slot today given the historic Q3 blowout that resets the HBM supply narrative. The dominant risk being underwritten is the AI capex ROI crisis — a hyperscaler guidance cut on capex would compress NVDA/AVGO/MU simultaneously — partially offset by the defensive sleeve's gold and Treasury duration hedges.
diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA, MU, AVGO — 3 names, max allowed; complementary value-chain positions in GPU, HBM memory, and custom ASIC/software respectively), Communication Services (META, GOOGL), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX, PWR), and Energy (XOM). Defensive sleeve spans 5 distinct exposures per the mandate: gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), wide-moat conglomerate cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — no exposure is doubled.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the irreplaceable toll road of the AI capex supercycle — Vera Rubin server ramp, Blackwell Ultra demand, and sovereign AI deployments globally underpin the earnings revision cycle. At ~$210 today, the stock has consolidated from its highs and Nvidia just completed a $25B multi-tranche debt offering signaling management confidence in long-term infrastructure investment. With AI-infrastructure beneficiaries expected to account for roughly half of all S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026, NVDA is the highest-conviction single name. Tiger Global holds it as a core position.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings report (est. late August) with Vera Rubin server ramp update and Blackwell Ultra shipment guidance
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$210, consolidating well off recent highs; ~46% analyst-consensus upside per TipRanks screen; accumulating above $200 50dma support is the disciplined entry zone
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler capex guidance cuts from two or more of MSFT/AMZN/META/GOOGL in a single quarter, or data center revenue growth decelerates below 100% YoY
picks.keyRisk: AI capex monetization collapse — hyperscalers pause buildout if AI ROI fails to materialize, triggering synchronized de-rating across the AI supply chain

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire added 224% to its Alphabet position in Q1 2026 — pushing GOOGL to the #7 holding at ~$16.6B — the single most striking smart-money signal of the season, with Greg Abel making it one of his first bold bets. At ~$371, Google Cloud's 28%+ growth and Search AI Overviews monetization provide dual-engine earnings revision upside. World-class ~58% gross margins, improving ROIC trajectory, and best risk-adjusted profile in Mag-7 make this a core carry-over. Antitrust (AdX divestiture ruling) is the key overhang — sized at 10% to reflect that risk.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Google Cloud revenue trajectory and AI Overviews monetization update — plus DoJ adtech remedies ruling expected 2H 2026
picks.entryNote: At ~$371, trades at ~22x fwd EPS — reasonable given 15-20% EPS growth trajectory; well below MSFT/META multiples on comparable cloud/AI growth; consolidating above 50dma
picks.exitTrigger: Federal court mandates AdX forced divestiture AND Google Cloud growth decelerates below 20% YoY in the same quarter
picks.keyRisk: Forced structural divestiture of AdX search advertising business, which would compress earnings power and set antitrust cascade precedent for other Mag-7 names

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Tepper nearly doubled his Amazon stake and made it his top holding; Ackman moved it to #2 at Pershing Square; Tiger Global holds it as a core position — the highest concentration of smart-money validation in the universe. AWS is now growing 28-29% YoY driving operating margin expansion, and Amazon is reportedly exploring selling custom AI chips to other companies, expanding its TAM beyond AWS. At ~$246, the margin re-rating thesis from retail leverage and AWS operating income is the fundamental driver; FY26 EPS estimates revised higher across brokerages.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — AWS revenue growth rate and first disclosure of Trainium chip external sales economics
picks.entryNote: At ~$246, below its 52-week high of $278.56; consolidating after a 25%+ recovery from the February $196 low; entering from a higher base is preferable — wait for pullbacks toward $235-240 if possible
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY or retail operating margins revert below 4% as AI capex burns FCF
picks.keyRisk: FCF turns deeply negative in 2026 if hyperscaler AI capex accelerates faster than AWS revenue monetization, compressing equity valuation

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META carries the strongest margin profile among Big Tech, with EPS of $7.14 vs. $5.75 consensus — a massive beat — and HSBC raised its price target to $900. AI-powered Advantage+ ad system is generating measurable ROI for advertisers, and Llama 4 monetization via API/enterprise is the next leg of the thesis. At ~$600, institutional accumulation is strong ahead of Llama monetization catalysts. This is the best-valued AI application layer name with a 35%+ operating margin and no regulatory binary imminent vs. GOOGL.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Advantage+ revenue contribution disclosure and Llama 4 enterprise API pricing announcement
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$600; HSBC $900 PT implies 50%+ upside; forward P/E roughly 25x on strong EPS growth trajectory — justified given margin profile and AI monetization optionality; near 50dma support
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY or Llama 4 fails to attract material enterprise contracts, suggesting AI investment is not monetizing
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory/antitrust cascade triggered by Alphabet AdX ruling extending to Meta's ad-market dominance, compressing ad pricing power

picks.name: The Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman's Q1 2026 investment banking revenue surged 48% — the clearest proof that M&A and capital markets are thawing after years of suppression. At a forward P/E of 15.24x, GS trades at a 27% discount to its sector average. The bear steepener yield curve directly expands NIM for wholesale banks, and deregulatory tailwinds from the 'One Big Beautiful Bill' support buybacks and capital return. As the preeminent capital markets franchise globally, GS is the highest-beta beneficiary of the M&A/IPO reactivation cycle. Persistent carry-over from the prior week given intact thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — investment banking revenue and trading revenue line items vs. elevated consensus expectations
picks.entryNote: Forward P/E 15.24x at 27% discount to sector; near 52-week high but fundamentals justify it — NIM expansion and capital markets re-rating are secular, not cyclical; trading above all key moving averages
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenue reverts to flat YoY growth for two consecutive quarters or credit spreads widen above 150 bps signaling credit stress
picks.keyRisk: Sharp reversal in capital markets activity — geopolitical escalation or Fed hike execution could freeze M&A pipelines and compress trading revenues simultaneously

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a high-quality compounder benefiting from three simultaneous tailwinds: (1) bond issuance volume surge as corporate debt needs refinancing in a structurally higher-rate world, boosting Ratings revenue; (2) the approved spin-off of the Mobility division in May 2026 which incrementally unlocks value for shareholders; and (3) AI-driven demand for Market Intelligence data products and Benchmarks. RBC reiterated Buy with a $495 price target in May 2026. Near-monopoly ratings moat, 85%+ subscription revenues, and expanding FCF yield make this a pristine quality carry-over.
picks.catalyst: Mobility Division spin-off completion (H2 2026) and Q2 earnings (late July) — Ratings issuance volume in a high-yield and IG market both running hot
picks.entryNote: Buy rating reiterated with $495 PT; subscription-model revenue base provides earnings floor; valuation near mid-cycle average on a sum-of-parts basis post-Mobility separation
picks.exitTrigger: Primary bond issuance dries up (IG + HY monthly volumes fall >40% YoY for two consecutive months) or Mobility spin-off delayed beyond Q1 2027
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory scrutiny of ratings duopoly (SPGI + MCO) intensifying, or a sharp credit market freeze that halts bond issuance volumes

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a dual-engine compounder with record backlog in both Raytheon defense (benefiting from structurally elevated NATO spending and Middle East tensions) and Collins Aerospace commercial aftermarket. Strong demand in both commercial and defense sectors is driving record backlog and robust sales, with long-term defense agreements and aftermarket strength underpin a positive multi-year outlook. A newly expanded landing gear facility in Poland signals NATO-aligned reshoring capex beneficiary. Defense spending is structurally elevated for 3-5+ years. This is a clean, low-risk industrial exposure with pricing power.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Raytheon missile systems backlog update and Collins Aerospace aftermarket revenue momentum
picks.entryNote: Earnings slides posted May 29, 2026 confirming strong fundamentals; trading at ~20x fwd EPS — reasonable for a quality industrial with secular defense tailwind; above all key moving averages
picks.exitTrigger: A comprehensive US-Iran peace deal or NATO defense spending commitments drop below 2% GDP on average, reducing the structural demand for Raytheon missiles and Collins avionics
picks.keyRisk: Defense budget reconciliation risk — if the 'One Big Beautiful Bill' deficit concerns force Pentagon budget cuts to offset tax reductions, Raytheon backlog growth stalls

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom reported Q2 FY2026 revenue of $22.2B (+48% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue up 143% to $10.8B — record fundamentals. The post-earnings pullback of ~14-20% from highs represents the post-reset entry the momentum screen flagged as more attractive. A Broadcom insider made a fresh multi-million dollar share purchase on June 15, 2026, a strong inside signal at the reset level. Custom ASIC/XPU moat for hyperscaler AI chips (Google TPU, Meta MTIA) is a durable competitive advantage that NVDA cannot easily displace. Analysts maintain Strong Buy consensus with $523.52 average target.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (September 2026) — AI semiconductor revenue trajectory and VMware infrastructure software integration margin update

phaseData.macro:
---

# US Equity Macro Regime Briefing — Friday, June 19, 2026

---

## 📊 Data Dashboard

**Fed Policy**

The Federal Reserve held the federal funds rate at **3.50%–3.75%** at its June 17, 2026 meeting.
 
The updated dot plot was the real shock: nine of eighteen officials now expect at least one rate hike by year-end — a stark reversal from March's cut-leaning stance — and rate-hike odds nearly doubled overnight to **84%**.
 
The June SEP revised median PCE inflation to **3.6%** (from 2.7% in March) and core PCE to **3.3%** (from 2.7%), both well above the 2% target.
 The neutral rate (r*) is estimated near 2.5–3.0%, putting the **real policy rate at approximately +0.5% to +1.0%** — nominally restrictive, but less so given the inflation overshoot. 
Markets have effectively priced out any 2026 cut, with Nuveen now expecting the first move — still a cut — in H1 2027.


**Yield Curve**

As of June 17, the curve is upward-sloping: 2Y at **4.20%**, 5Y at **4.27%**, 10Y at **4.49%**, 30Y at **4.93%**. The 2s10s spread is **+29 bps** and the 3m10y spread is **+73 bps**.
 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to buoy longer maturities.
 After years of inversion, the curve has re-normalized in a **bear steepener** fashion — not a bull steepener — which is the uncomfortable flavor driven by long-end supply and inflation risk premia, not by Fed easing.

**Inflation**

The separately reported May CPI ran hot at **4.2% year-over-year**.
 
Officials projected PCE price pressures to prove more persistent than previously expected, with headline PCE seen ending 2026 at **3.6%** and core PCE at **3.3%**.
 
Oil futures markets are pricing roughly **$85/barrel** through year-end, and tech-related mismeasurement continues to inflate core CPI readings
 — both factors keeping "supercore" (services ex-housing) re-accelerating rather than cooling. Trajectory: **re-accelerating / sticky**, not decelerating.

**Growth**

Per the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, real GDP growth is currently tracking at approximately **3% QoQ annualized** for Q2.
 
Payrolls have averaged approximately **190,000 per month** over the past three months, signaling a firmer labor market.
 
ISM Manufacturing PMI registered **52.7**, anchored by an increase in new orders as businesses rushed to avoid shortages from geopolitical disruption.
 
The Fed trimmed projected 2026 growth to **2.2%** and nudged its unemployment forecast to **4.3%**.


**Credit & Liquidity**

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **2.78%** (278 bps) in June 2026.
 
Broad IG OAS is running around **~80 bps**, with tighter spreads for AA issuers and wider for BBB.
 Both are well inside recessionary levels, suggesting credit is still in a risk-on posture even as equities wobbled post-FOMC. 
The DXY firmed near **100.80** following the hawkish FOMC, a level not seen since May 2025, after the Fed removed its reference to "additional rate adjustments."


**Earnings Backdrop**

Goldman Sachs raised its S&P 500 year-end 2026 forecast to **8,000** and lifted its 2026 EPS estimate to **$340** (representing ~24% annual growth).
 
AI-infrastructure beneficiaries are expected to account for roughly **half of all S&P 500 earnings growth** this year.
 
The valuation multiple is expected to hold flat at roughly **~21x forward earnings**, as modest yield declines are offset by slowing growth, geopolitical uncertainty, and investor skepticism about AI profit durability.
 The 10-year average forward P/E is approximately 17–18x, making current valuations roughly **3–4 turns rich** on a historical basis. 
Forward P/E is near a level not seen since the internet bubble of 2000.


---

## **1. Regime Call: Late-Cycle**

**This is a late-cycle regime masquerading as mid-cycle.** The combination of re-accelerating inflation, a Fed that just flipped its dot plot from cuts to a potential hike, real rates that are barely restrictive relative to 4.2% CPI, 
front-loaded hawkishness with materially higher near-term inflation alongside slightly softer growth
, a labor market running hot at ~190K/month, and credit spreads still compressed defines the textbook late-cycle configuration — not early-cycle broadening. The bull steepener everyone hoped for never arrived; instead the curve re-normalized from the long end up (bear steepener), which historically compresses multiples and rewards quality over beta.

---

## **2. What's Working / What's Breaking**

**What's working:** 
Semiconductor companies remain the primary direct beneficiaries of the AI capex supercycle, and tech hardware, industrials, and utilities companies are also receiving a large earnings boost from the AI buildout.
 Energy is a standout — 
with oil futures pricing roughly $85/barrel through year-end
, E&P and integrated majors benefit from both elevated realizations and inflation pass-through. 
Only the Energy sector has notable breadth in terms of contributing earnings stocks.
 Financials benefit from a steepening curve and wider NIM. Short-duration, high-quality credit (IG over HY at these spread levels) outperforms as rate volatility stays elevated.

**What's breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors — residential REITs, homebuilders, utilities with heavy debt loads — are structurally challenged with the 30Y at 4.93%. 
Real wage growth is negative, even as the total payroll level remains elevated
, which is beginning to crimp consumer discretionary. Small-caps (Russell 2000) are a trap: floating-rate debt burdens are rising as any rate-cut catalyst evaporates. 
The AI "circular financing" problem is acute: the very companies that need continued hyperscaler capex to justify their earnings trajectories are the same companies whose results depend on one another's spending.
 Mega-cap tech concentration risk is elevated. Long-duration growth at any price is dead money in a world where the Fed is threatening hikes, not cuts.

---

## **3. What to Watch — Top 2–3 Highest-Impact Events**

1. **June CPI (release ~July 10) & May PCE (July 30):** 
Every incoming CPI print and PCE revision now carries more weight because the Fed has explicitly refused to anchor expectations between meetings.
 A June CPI above 4.0% YoY or a core PCE re-acceleration toward 3.5%+ would likely trigger immediate repricing of a July or September hike, sending 2Y yields through 4.50% and compressing equity multiples sharply. A surprise deceleration is the single biggest positive catalyst.

2. **July 29–30 FOMC Meeting:** 
The committee is more worried about inflation running hot than about the labor market cracking — the configuration that keeps policy restrictive rather than easing.
 With nine dots projecting a hike, 
short-term Treasury yields may rise further over the near term.
 Any language shift back toward cuts is a powerful equity tailwind; any hike execution would be the most significant negative macro shock of the cycle.

3. **Geopolitical / Fiscal Tape — Middle East & US Debt Ceiling/Budget Reconciliation:** 
ISM Manufacturing is already being distorted by businesses rushing to build inventory ahead of supply disruptions from geopolitical conflict
, and 
oil markets are pricing $85/barrel through year-end
 partly on this basis. Any escalation pushes energy costs higher, re-ignites headline CPI, and eliminates any residual dovish optionality for the Fed. Separately, the Congressional budget reconciliation process (tax cuts + deficit expansion) poses an upside supply shock to long-end Treasuries — the 30Y at 4.93% could test 5.25%+ if the fiscal trajectory deteriorates, which would be the most direct threat to the 21x forward multiple holding.

---
*All data sourced from FOMC June 17, 2026 SEP, StreetStats, Nuveen, Goldman Sachs, Schwab, and Federal Reserve/FRED. Data as of June 17–19, 2026.*

### 🟢 2. BERKSHIRE HATHAWAY (BRK.B) — All-Weather Compunder at Reasonable Valuation
**Thesis: Massive cash fortress, diversified earnings, no binary risk**

BRK.B checks every box: Buffett has been building cash reserves throughout 2025–26, positioning the firm to deploy capital opportunistically in a correction. For income-focused investors, SCHD's 2026 showing makes a strong case.

### 🟢 4. COSTCO (COST) — Consumer Staples-Adjacent Compounder With Pricing Power
**Thesis: Membership model, recession-resilient, low-beta, durable moat**

COST is a structural winner across economic cycles: its membership-driven revenue model (near 90%+ renewal rates) creates sticky, recurring revenue that is uncorrelated with discretionary macro. The warehouse model gives it cost advantages that are structurally difficult for competitors to replicate. While COST's P/E is never "cheap," its multiple is well-supported by the certainty of its cash flows. It tends to see traffic *accelerate* in recessions as consumers trade down from premium retailers. In a risk-off tape, COST holds better than virtually any growth name in this universe.

---

### 🟢 5. IEF (iShares 7–10 Year Treasury ETF) — Duration Hedge With Important Caveat

## SECTION 1 — SINGLE-STOCK BLOW-UP RISK
### 5 Names with Highest Probability of –25%+ Drawdown (Next 6–12 Months)

---

### 🔴 1. TSLA — Tesla, Inc.
**The problem: A car company priced as if it already won the AI wars**


At roughly 180x trailing earnings and over 14x trailing revenue, Tesla's stock price embeds years of successful execution on robotaxi, Optimus, and energy scaling.
 The operating data does not yet underwrite this narrative. 
Deliveries were 1.64 million vehicles in 2025 (–8.6% YoY), with Q4 deliveries of 418,227 (–16% YoY), missing even reduced expectations.



A forward P/E near 190 times leaves limited room for a delayed Cybercab ramp, a regulatory setback in autonomy, or a demand slowdown in the automotive segment.
 The drawdown pathway is well-mapped: 
the path to $190 runs through three catalysts over the next 12 months: a Q2 or Q3 delivery miss, a margin reset once warranty and tariff benefits roll off, and a Robotaxi or Optimus timeline slip.


Key risk flags:
- 
Consensus forecasts for TSLA's EV volumes and margins appear overly optimistic given intensifying global competition and fading EV credits. Key growth drivers — robotaxi and Optimus — are delayed, highly competitive, and unlikely to justify current multiples before 2030.

- 
The FSD timeline has slipped repeatedly, and any further delay creates earnings pressure from a multiple that requires autonomy to arrive soon. Elon Musk's divided attention across Tesla, SpaceX, and X adds execution risk that the current premium does not obviously price in.

- 
Absent a clean Cybercab ramp, durable 22%-plus automotive gross margins, and FSD monetization scaling, the stock is priced for a future the operating numbers are not yet underwriting.


**Blow-up vector:** Any negative NHTSA ruling, Cybercab production delay, or Q2 delivery miss below the 450K–475K consensus band → multiple collapses toward industrial-auto comps (~20–25x). –30% to –50% scenario is credible.

---

### 🔴 2. PLTR — Palantir Technologies
**The problem: Extraordinary business growth still can't justify a 77x forward P/E**


The P/E ratio for Palantir Technologies (PLTR) is 134.63 as of June 22, 2026, representing a decrease of –66% compared to its 12-month average P/E ratio of 395.97
 — a sign of how violently this stock reprices when sentiment shifts.


The current Forward P/E Ratio is 77.48, which is 334.1% above the software industry median of 17.85.
 Even the bear-friendly intrinsic value analysis is damning: 
the intrinsic value for PLTR under the Base Case is $45.7 USD; compared with the current market price of $118.38 USD, the stock appears overvalued by 61%.



Despite robust operating trends, the stock has faced selling pressure and valuation pushback, with trailing P/E above 150x, forward P/S over 40, insider share sales, regulatory scrutiny in the UK and Switzerland, and concerns about competition from larger AI players.


**Blow-up vector:** Any growth deceleration (the current >80% YoY revenue growth rate is mathematically unsustainable), a government contract non-renewal, or a broad AI multiple compression event. The stock trades like a call option — it can lose 40–60% without the business fundamentally changing.

---

### 🔴 3. NVDA — NVIDIA Corporation
**The problem: Regulatory overhang on China + $5.4T market cap priced for perpetual dominance**


As of the end of fiscal year 2026, NVIDIA was effectively foreclosed from competing in China's data center computing/compute market, and this effective foreclosure helped competitors build larger developer and customer ecosystems to challenge them worldwide.



China's State Administration for Market Regulation announced in September 2025 that NVIDIA violated its 2020 antitrust review terms. Beijing has directed domestic companies to prioritize local chip alternatives and effectively blacklisted the H20. Even approved H200 sales face a 25% U.S. import tariff, adding another layer of friction.



A further $8 billion in expected H20 revenue was set to disappear in Q2. In December 2025, the Trump administration approved sales of the more advanced H200 chip to China, but attached a condition requiring the U.S. government to receive 25% of resulting revenue. Those sales have since stalled completely. As of the most recent earnings call, NVIDIA had generated zero revenue under the H200 program.


The gross margin damage is already visible: 
on April 9, 2025, NVIDIA was informed that a license is required for H20 exports to China. As a result, NVIDIA incurred a $4.5 billion charge in Q1 FY2026 associated with H20 excess inventory and purchase obligations.


**Blow-up vector:** A tightening of export controls to cover Blackwell-class chips, a hyperscaler capex pullback, or a credible Chinese domestic GPU (Huawei Ascend) gaining meaningful share. Despite the enormous growth story, at ~33x P/E the stock is not cheap for a cyclical semiconductor. A capex slowdown from any of the top 4 hyperscalers creates a cascading downgrade cycle.

---

### 🔴 4. SNOW — Snowflake, Inc.
**The problem: Cloud data warehouse under competitive assault, priced for perfection**


Investors wanting AI exposure without concentrating their entire position at 80x trailing sales
 should treat SNOW as a cautionary tale. The stock's valuation has historically been one of the most stretched in the software sector. The competitive threat is structural: AWS Redshift, Google BigQuery, Databricks, and now AI-native query tools are commoditizing exactly what Snowflake charges premium prices for. Revenue growth is decelerating from triple digits toward the mid-20s%, while consumption-based pricing creates top-line volatility directly tied to enterprise budget cycles.

**Blow-up vector:** An enterprise IT spending pause (common in recessionary or rate-shock environments), a major customer churn event to Databricks or a hyperscaler-native alternative, or further contraction in software multiples. –30% to –40% drawdown is achievable in a sector rotation out of high-multiple SaaS.

---

### 🔴 5. CELH — Celsius Holdings
**The problem: A single-channel, single-product story at a growth premium with deteriorating fundamentals**

Celsius is structurally exposed to customer concentration risk (Pepsi distribution agreement represents a dominant revenue share), a decelerating energy drink category after post-COVID hypergrowth, and the stock ran on multiple expansion, not durable earnings growth. International expansion is early-stage and unproven. Gross margins, while healthy, face compression as promotional spending intensifies against Monster and Red Bull incumbents.

**Blow-up vector:** Any guidance cut, Pepsi renegotiation, or category deceleration. The stock is a classic "hypergrowth + high multiple + deteriorating fundamental momentum" triple-threat blow-up candidate. –25% to –50% drawdown is well within historical range for this archetype.

## SECTION 2 — DEFENSIVE OPPORTUNITIES
### 5 Names Combining Durable Moat + Reasonable Valuation + Low-Correlation Behavior

---

### 🟢 1. GLD — SPDR Gold Shares ETF
**The anchor in a fragile world**


Upside exposure in gold and downside in the USD may help to protect against institutional risks resurfacing.
 Gold is currently the clearest cross-asset hedge against multiple simultaneous tail risks: a fiscal doom loop in U.S. Treasuries, USD debasement, geopolitical escalation (the Iran conflict backdrop is live), and AI capex disappointment. 
Inflation is proving sticky, keeping rates higher for longer, with the 10-year Treasury now expected to remain in a 4.0%–4.8% range. Three key risks are emerging: "AI capex vigilantes" as investors demand returns on heavy AI spending, fading cyclical tailwinds, and persistent upward pressure on interest rates.
 In all three of those environments, gold historically outperforms. The GLD ETF at ~$428 has institutional depth, 0DTE options for hedging, and zero credit risk.

---

### 🟢 2. BRK.B — Berkshire Hathaway Class B
**The ultimate risk-off compounder**

Berkshire holds ~$330B+ in cash/T-bills (earning ~$18B/year in risk-free income), owns BNSF railroad, GEICO, Berkshire Energy, and an enormous equity portfolio anchored in AAPL and BAC. It has a natural hedge: when markets fall, Buffett deploys. Valuation is reasonable (~1.5x book). In a risk-off tape, BRK.B historically demonstrates low drawdown because: (1) cash pile acts as an automatic stabilizer, (2) insurance premiums are counter-cyclical, (3) no earnings dependence on AI capex narratives. 
Wide-moat stocks have been less volatile, and no-moat stocks the most volatile. The wide-moat index consistently held up best during down markets.
 BRK.B is the canonical wide-moat defensive.

---

### 🟢 3. LLY — Eli Lilly & Co.
**Secular growth story at a more defensible valuation post-correction**

LLY has pulled back meaningfully from its 2024 peak, making its GLP-1 (Mounjaro/Zepbound) dominance more reasonably priced. The obesity and diabetes drug market is a genuine $150B+ secular opportunity. LLY has manufacturing scale, patent exclusivity through the late 2030s, and pipeline depth (donanemab for Alzheimer's). In a risk-off tape, pharma with visible multi-year earnings growth is a "quality growth" hideout. 
2026 is expected to bring increased focus on affordability challenges such as health care and electricity prices
 — which keeps GLP-1 drugs politically hot but commercially indomitable. Healthcare broadly shows low correlation to AI/tech sell-offs.

---

### 🟢 4. SCHD — Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
**The income fortress**

SCHD screens for dividend growth, free cash flow coverage, and return on equity — producing a portfolio of companies like Chevron, Coca-Cola, Cisco, Lockheed, and Home Depot. Current yield ~3.5–4.0%, with a beta to the S&P 500 meaningfully below 1.0. In a rate-volatile, risk-off environment, SCHD's combination of income, low P/E holdings, and sector diversification (energy, financials, industrials, staples) provides genuine ballast. 
Earnings are driving the bull market, but market leadership is narrow and concentrated in AI and energy-related sectors. Markets may be vulnerable to disappointment with stretched positioning, a thin equity risk premium, and rising bond yield pressure.
 SCHD thrives precisely when stretched-multiple AI names compress.

---

### 🟢 5. IEF — iShares 7–10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
**Tactical duration in a growth-scare scenario**


AI-related credit expansion, wider fiscal deficits, and persistent external imbalances do not mean that U.S. Treasuries have lost their hedging role in multi-asset portfolios.
 IEF (7–10 year duration) is the sweet spot: enough duration to benefit from a Fed pivot or growth scare, without the extreme sensitivity of TLT to a fiscal/bond-vigilante scenario. 
For shorter-term bonds, yields may decline as labor markets soften further and the Fed adopts a more dovish stance, either in response to higher unemployment or a more dovish composition starting in 2026.
 IEF currently yields ~4.3–4.4% — offering genuine carry while providing equity-portfolio insurance against a macro slowdown. Unlike TLT, it is less exposed to the fiscal tail risk where long-end yields spike on deficit concerns.
